title: Multilingual Language Model Pretraining usingMachine-translated Data
authors: Jiayi Wangα∗Yao Luα∗Maurice WeberβMax RyabininβDavid AdelaniγYihong ChenαRaphael TangPontus Stenetorpα,δαCentre for Artificial Intelligence, University College LondonβTogether AIγMila, McGill University, Canada CIFAR AI ChairδResearch and Development Center for Large Language Models, National Institute of Informatics{jiaywang,yao.lu,yihong.chen,p.stenetorp}@cs.ucl.ac.uk,{maurice,mryab}@together.ai,david.adelani@mila.quebec
abstract: High-resource languages such as English, enables the pretraining of high-quality large language models (LLMs). The same can not be said for most other languages as LLMs still underperform for non-English languages, likely due to a gap in the quality and diversity of the available multilingual pretraining corpora. In this work, we find that machine-translated texts from a single high-quality source language can contribute significantly to the pretraining quality of multilingual LLMs. We translate FineWeb-Edu, a high-quality English web dataset, into nine languages, resulting in a 1.71.7-trillion-token dataset, which we call TransWebEdu and pretrain a 1.31.3B-parameter model, TransWebLLM, from scratch on this dataset. Across nine non-English reasoning tasks, we show that TransWebLLM matches or outperforms state-of-the-art multilingual models trained using closed data, such as Llama3.2, Qwen2.5, and Gemma, despite using an order of magnitude less data. We demonstrate that adding less than 5% of TransWebEdu as domain-specific pretraining data sets a new state-of-the-art in Arabic, Italian, Indonesian, Swahili, and Welsh understanding and commonsense reasoning tasks. To promote reproducibility, we release our corpus, models, and training pipeline under Open Source Initiative-approved licenses.

paragraphs: Multilingual language models have shown remarkable potential for natural language processing (Dubey et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025; Team et al., 2024), yet their development faces a fundamental challenge: the scarcity of high-quality training data across diverse languages Joshi et al. (2020); Kreutzer et al. (2022). While English benefits from extensive, diverse, and well-curated datasets, other languages—even widely spoken ones—struggle to match this standard. Current practices of collecting and filtering web data from the Internet lag behind English performance due to the Internet’s inherent English-centric nature (Bender et al., 2021; Imani et al., 2023).

One direction to address the data quality issue is using pretrained language models to generate high-quality synthetic data Maini et al. (2024); Abdin et al. (2024). However, language model-based data generation is not suitable for multilingual research due to limited language coverage. For example, one of the current strongest models with multilingual support, Llama 3.2 (Dubey et al., 2024), officially supports fewer than 20 languages. For low-resource languages like Welsh, the near-absence of well-supported large language models makes data generation practically impossible. Furthermore, even with proprietary language models such as GPT-4, which might have better coverage, generation at the trillion-token scale is non-trivial.111The cost would approximately exceed millions of dollars.

To address the limited language coverage issue, machine translation (MT) offers a potential solution for creating multilingual datasets using task-specific neural machine translation (NMT) models, with the goal of preserving contextual, idiomatic, and pragmatic nuances across languages. However, this line of research (Urbizu et al., 2023; Doshi et al., 2024) remains underexplored for pretraining.

In this work, we introduce TransWebEdu, a large-scale multilingual corpus created by translating a subset of FineWeb-edu (Lozhkov et al., 2024), a high-quality English dataset, into nine diverse languages using NLLB-200-1.3B (Costa-jussà et al., 2022). The resulting corpus spans ten languages—Arabic, French, German, Indonesian, Italian, Russian, Spanish, Swahili, Welsh, and English—exceeding 100100B tokens per language, with a total of 1.7 trillion tokens. We evaluate the efficiency of TransWebEdu by pretraining a 1.31.3B-parameter language model. Contrary to common concerns about text quality when using “small” sentence-level NMT models, we demonstrate that it can yield substantial improvements on pretraining performance. For instance, TransWebEdu improves Swahili by 10% over the SOTA Gemma (Team et al., 2024) and enhances Arabic and Italian, surpassing it by 5% and 2.6%, respectively.
In summary, our contributions are as follows:

1. We translate a high-quality, pretraining-scale English corpus into nine diverse languages, including three medium- and low-resource languages, using a sentence-level NMT model, creating one of the largest machine-generated multilingual datasets to date, TransWebEdu, containing 1.7T tokens.

2. We pretrain TransWebLLM, a 1.3B-parameter model, from scratch on our TransWebEdu dataset. The model establishes SOTA-level multilingual performance on a wide range of reasoning tasks across nine languages despite using significantly fewer tokens than leading models trained on closed-source data, including Gemma, Qwen2.5, and Llama3.2.
3. We release our corpus, models, and training pipeline under open licenses to advance reproducibility in multilingual NLP.

This section outlines our pipeline for constructing a machine-translated corpus and pretraining a multilingual language model on it. Our process consists of the following steps: (i) We select a high-quality English pretraining dataset; (ii) We segment English documents into sentences, translate each sentence into target languages using a sentence-level neural machine translation model, and reconstruct the documents by concatenating the translated sentences; (iii) We train a language model from scratch on the resulting multilingual data mixture and validate the effectiveness of the pretraining corpus.

subsections.title: 2.1 Pretraining Data Curation
paragraphs: LLMs are predominantly trained on document-level text data, as demonstrated by prominent model families such as Llama (Dubey et al., 2024) and Gemma (Team et al., 2024). In keeping with this established approach, we structure our translation pretraining data at the document level. Translation data typically comprises the following key components: source data, target languages, translation model, and a translation approach for composing document-level translations.

The quality of a pretraining dataset significantly influences the performance of LLMs. Among high-resource languages, English stands out for its vast linguistic diversity and extensive knowledge base (Joshi et al., 2020; Kreutzer et al., 2022). This makes it an excellent choice for sourcing high-quality web data for model training.

The FineWeb-Edu dataset (Lozhkov et al., 2024), a subset of FineWeb (Penedo et al., 2024), has demonstrated its quality and efficacy in the development of LLMs. This dataset, constructed using scalable automated high-quality annotations for educational value, has been instrumental in training both English-centric models like GPT-2 (Karpathy, 2022, 2024) and multilingual models such as EuroLLM (Martins et al., 2024), and thus emerges as a suitable candidate for our source dataset. It originally consists 1.31.3 trillions tokens of educational contents, and our focus is on the sample-100100BT subset222hf.co/datasets/HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-edu, which contains about 100100 billion GPT-2 tokens randomly sampled from the whole dataset.

We focus on 99 target languages from diverse linguistic families, ensuring broad representation. From the Indo-European family, we include Germanic languages: English (en) and German (de); Romance languages: French (fr), Spanish (es), and Italian (it); a Celtic language: Welsh (cy); and a Slavic language: Russian (ru). Additionally, we include languages from distinct families: Afroasiatic (Arabic (ar)), Niger-Congo (Swahili (sw)), and Austronesian (Indonesian (id)). According to Joshi et al. (2020) and Ezeani et al. (2019), Indonesian is categorized as a medium-resource language, while Swahili and Welsh are classified as low-resource languages. The remaining languages are considered high-resource. We translate the 100100BT FineWeb-Edu corpus from English into the target languages to bridge resource gaps and transfer the rich knowledge representations encoded in English data, ultimately enhancing the inclusion and representation of these target languages in multilingual LLMs.

Although various models support translation tasks, including neural machine translation (NMT) models and LLMs (Stahlberg, 2020; Alves et al., 2024; Martins et al., 2024). However, exploration of LLMs’ translation performance for medium- and low-resource languages remains limited. For example, TowerLLM (Alves et al., 2024), an open multilingual LLM designed for translation-related tasks, supports only 10 languages. In contrast, NMT models are generally more accessible and widely adopted for translation, particularly for low-resource languages, due to more than a decade of dedicated development in this field (Stahlberg, 2020). A prominent example is NLLB-200 (Costa-jussà et al., 2022), an open-source suit of models capable of providing high-quality translations for 200 languages. These models are purpose-built to support linguistic diversity and are optimized for robust performance, even for resource-scarce languages.

In our study, we assess whether constructing documents using sentence-level translations can still yield robust pretraining performance for LLMs. We hypothesize that essential linguistic patterns and semantic structures in high-quality source data can be preserved despite translation imperfections—an attribute that could be particularly beneficial for cold-start pretraining in low-resource languages. If successful, this method could greatly expand access to multilingual pretraining data.

Specifically, we begin by segmenting English documents into sentences with the NLTK sentence tokenizer (Bird et al., 2009) and then translate them into target languages using the NLLB-200 model (Costa-jussà et al., 2022). The translated sentences are reassembled into documents, with their original order and structural elements intact (e.g., newline characters)333The translation pipeline is detailed in Appendix A.. We employ NLLB-200-1.3B, the smallest non-distilled model in the NLLB-200 suite, trying to explore the lower bound of translation quality. While this model may not represent the absolute lower bound, it provides a practical reference point for assessing the impact of noisier translations on LLM pretraining.

Through the aforementioned approach, we obtain a machine-translated pretraining corpus covering 1010 languages in total. We refer to this multiway parallel translated dataset as TransWebEdu. The NLLB translation is performed with a batch size of 4096 and a beam size of 1444For each target language, the entire translation process is completed within 168 GPU hours using a single GH200 GPU.. Table 8 in Appendix B presents the statistics of the original English data alongside those of the translated components in TransWebEdu. To the best of our knowledge, TransWebEdu is the largest multiway parallel, document-level multilingual pretraining dataset currently available.

subsections.title: 2.2 Multilingual LM Pretraining
paragraphs: In this section, we will describe the technical details of pretraining multilingual language models using TransWebEdu.

We pretrain a multilingual language model from scratch using TransWebEdu, naming it TransWebLLM. The architecture and hyperparameter selection of TransWebLLM (detailed in Appendix Section C) are informed by insights from the Llama family of models and other open-source efforts Karpathy (2024) to reproduce GPT-2. TransWebLLM consists of 1.31.3B parameters in total. Similar to GPT-2 reproduction efforts, we adopt a constant learning rate of 6×10−46\times 10^{-4}, a sequence length of up to 20482048 tokens, and a batch size of 20482048 per iteration, resulting in approximately 44 million tokens processed per iteration.

Alves et al. (2024) extends the multilingual capabilities of Llama2 models (Touvron et al., 2023), demonstrating that the Llama2 tokenizer remains a practical choice for ensuring efficiency across diverse languages. Building on their findings, we use the Llama2 tokenizer in our experiments. For non-Latin languages, such as Arabic and Russian, it tokenizes the text while representing it using Unicode-based embeddings.

During pretraining, we use the same pretraining setup as GPT-family models, where documents are randomly sampled from the corpus. As a result, the likelihood of the same document appearing in different languages within a single batch is very low. For clarity, Table 1 presents an example of our pretraining data.

TransWebLLM is trained using the Megatron-LM framework (Shoeybi et al., 2019) from scratch with an accelerated attention implementation (Dao, 2023). The training is done on the NVIDIA GH200 cluster (McIntosh-Smith et al., 2024) for 8,3668,366 GPU hours. Our pretraining dataset TransWebEdu is created on a balanced basis, so we do not perform any up-sampling for specific languages. Our pretraining over TransWebEdu processes approximately 1.51.5T tokens, which is nearly one epoch of the pretraining data. Similar to the observation of Muennighoff et al. (2024), we observe no degradation in performance over the validation set during this process.
This section presents our evaluation of model performance across various multilingual benchmarks.

subsections.title: 3.1 Evaluation Benchmark Datasets
paragraphs: Our evaluation includes English benchmarks and assessments for the nine non-English languages, focusing on natural language understanding and common-sense reasoning. All benchmarks used are publicly available and open-source, ensuring the transparency and reproducibility of our results555All evaluations are conducted using https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness..

Specifically, our evaluation framework includes the following tasks: ARC (Clark et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2023; Bayes et al., 2024): grade-school level multiple-choice science questions; Hellaswag (Zellers et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2023): common-sense reasoning benchmarks for contextually appropriate sentence endings prediction; PAWS-X (Yang et al., 2019): a cross-lingual adversarial dataset for paraphrase identification, sourced from English Wikipedia and Quora; PIQA (Bisk et al., 2020): physical commonsense reasoning benchmarks; SciQ (Johannes Welbl, 2017): an multiple-choice question-answering dataset in scientific topics; TruthfulQA (Lin et al., 2021a; Bayes et al., 2024): question-answering evaluation tasks for truthfulness and factual accuracy of model responses; XCOPA (Ponti et al., 2020): across-lingual adaptation of COPA (Roemmele et al., 2011) for transfer commonsense reasoning evaluation; XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018): an multilingual extension of Williams et al. (2018), assessing textual entailment prediction; XStoryCloze (Lin et al., 2021b): an multilingual adaptation of Mostafazadeh et al. (2016) for assessing cross-lingual narrative understanding by predicting story endings; XWinograd (Tikhonov and Ryabinin, 2021): a cross-lingual adaptation of the Winograd Schema challenge666https://cs.nyu.edu/~davise/papers/WinogradSchemas/WS.html for coreference resolution evaluation. However, not all ten languages have all the above benchmarks publicly available. The specific evaluation datasets used for each language are detailed in Table 10 in Appendix D777For Welsh evaluation, we use the BritEval benchmarks, https://llm.org.uk/.. All benchmarks are evaluated using a standard 55-shot setting, with results reported in terms of accuracy.

subsections.title: 3.2 Baselines
paragraphs: We benchmark our TransWebLLM, trained with TransWebEdu, in a 55-shot setting, against diverse open-source multilingual and monolingual LLMs with comparable parameter sizes, different multilingual pretraining mixtures and data sources.

Our multilingual LLM baselines include: mGPT (1.31.3B) (Shliazhko et al., 2022), BLOOM (1.11.1B) (Workshop et al., 2022), Llama3.2 (1.31.3B) (Dubey et al., 2024), Qwen2 (1.51.5B) (Yang et al., 2024), Qwen2.5 (1.51.5B) (Yang et al., 2025), and Gemma (2.62.6B) (Team et al., 2024). Additionally, we compare against language-specific LLM baselines: Afriteva_v2_large (11B) (Oladipo et al., 2023) for Swahili, BritLLM (33B)888 hf.co/britllm/britllm-3b-v0.1 for Welsh, CroissantLLM (1.31.3B) (Faysse et al., 2024) for French, EuroLLM (1.71.7B) (Martins et al., 2024) for Arabic, French, German, Italian, Russian, and Spanish, Jais-family-1p3b (1.31.3B) (Sengupta et al., 2023) for Arabic, Sailor (1.81.8B) (Dou et al., 2024a) and Sailor2 (11B) (Dou et al., 2024b) for Indonesian. Furthermore, we include two English-centric baselines in our evaluation: TinyLlama (1.11.1B) (Zhang et al., 2024), Pythia (1.41.4B) (Biderman et al., 2023). An overview of baseline models and our TransWebLLM has been shown in Table 11 in Appendix E.

subsections.title: 3.3 Main results ofTransWebLLM
paragraphs: Table 2 presents the average performance of TransWebLLM across benchmark datasets for each language, comparing it to baseline models across all 10 languages. The last three columns of Table 2 summarize the overall average performance for (i) All languages, (ii) Non-English languages, and (iii) High-resource languages. Generally, TransWebLLM consistently ranks among the top three models in terms of average performance across all languages and non-English languages, with the accuracy scores of 45.0845.08 and 43.8343.83, respectively. It significantly outperforms multilingual LLMs such as mGPT, BLOOM, Llama3.2, Qwen2, and Qwen2.5 of similar model size, and achieves comparable results with Gemma, despite Gemma having twice the model size of TransWebLLM.

For high-resource languages, TransWebLLM ranks among the top three models for Arabic, German, and Italian. Additionally, it outperforms Llama3.2 on average (45.7345.73 vs. 43.8943.89), despite the latter being trained on 9T tokens—an order of magnitude more than the data used for training TransWebLLM. A similar trend is observed in French evaluation when comparing TransWebLLM to CroissantLLM. TransWebLLM outperforms CroissantLLM (46.1146.11 vs. 45.7345.73), even though the latter is trained on 3T tokens, with half of them in French. In contrast, TransWebLLM is trained on 1.51.5T tokens, with less than 1010% (150150B) in French, primarily consisting of translated data.

More remarkable results are observed in medium- and low-resource languages. For Indonesian, TransWebLLM outperforms both Qwen2 and Qwen2.5, despite the latter two being trained on 77T and 1818T tokens. The most notable gains are seen in Swahili and Welsh, where TransWebLLM ranks first among all baselines, achieving accuracy scores of 44.4444.44 and 38.6938.69, outperforming Gemma (2.62.6B) and BritLLM (33B). These results suggest that training with translation data can be a viable cold-start strategy for pretraining LLMs in medium- and low-resource languages.

For English, TransWebLLM performs slightly worse than TinyLlama but outperforms CroissantLLM. Despite being trained on significantly less English data—only 150150B tokens compared to TinyLlama’s 33T and CroissantLLM’s 1.51.5T—TransWebLLM achieves competitive performance, underscoring the importance of the high-quality English source.

In this section, we explore (1) the impact of LLM-generated translation data on pretraining performance and (2) the effects of additional data sources, including general web data and specialized datasets such as rephrased synthetic text, code, and instruction data. We address these questions through a series of ablation experiments.

subsections.title: 4.1 Pretraining with Translation Data generated from an LLM
paragraphs: Recent studies (Alves et al., 2024; Martins et al., 2024) show that LLMs can effectively support translation tasks, raising the question: How does pretraining performance differ when using a translation corpus generated by an LLM versus an NMT model like NLLB, used in TransWebLLM?

Dubey et al. (2024) highlighted Mistral’s potential for multilingual NLP, while Moslem et al. (2023) and Kocmi et al. (2024) demonstrated its effectiveness in machine translation. Based on these insights, we use Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1999hf.co/mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 for translation, and we focus on English, French, German, and Spanish for this ablation. Details on data generation are provided in the Appendix F. A key difference between Mistral- and NLLB-generated translations lies in their approach to text segmentation. Mistral is prompted to translate chunked documents, better preserving contextual coherence, whereas NLLB translates at the sentence level, which may result in inconsistencies in document-level fluency and cohesion.

Due to computational capacity, we translate 6464B tokens from the sample-100100BT subset of FineWeb-Edu. We then pretrain an LLM from scratch using the same training framework as TransWebLLM, naming it CuatroLLM. For a fair comparison, we extract the corresponding NLLB-translated data for French, German, and Spanish from TransWebEdu and retrain TransWebLLM using this subset, referring to the resulting model as TransWebLLM-4.

Both CuatroLLM and TransWebLLM-4 are evaluated on benchmark datasets for English, French, German, and Spanish, as introduced in Section 3.1, with results presented in Table 13 in Appendix F. Across four languages, TransWebLLM and CuatroLLM perform similarly (46.6546.65 vs. 47.1647.16), both surpassing mGPT, BLOOM, and Llama3.2 on average. This suggests that the choice of translation method has a limited impact on these specific downstream evaluations. However, a key advantage of the NLLB-200 model—with support for 200 languages—is its scalability and efficiency, making it a more viable choice for expanding multilingual pretraining to a broader range of languages.

subsections.title: 4.2 Beyond Pretraining with Translation Data
paragraphs: In this section, we assess whether adding specialized data provide benefits beyond pretraining with machine-translated data.

Given that TransWebEdu is largely based on educational contents, which is a highly specialized domain, we investigate whether multilingual reasoning capabilities can be further enhanced by incorporating general web data.

We construct the general web dataset by sampling English, French, German, Italian, and Spanish data from RedPajama-v2 (RPv2) (Weber et al., 2024); Arabic, Russian, and Indonesian from mC4 (Xue et al., 2021); Swahili from Wura (Oladipo et al., 2023); and Welsh from CC100 (Wenzek et al., 2020). For RPv2, we filter each subset using its built-in quality signals101010Details are introduced in Appendix G.; for mC4, we apply random sampling. Given the limited availability of Swahili and Welsh data in Wura and CC100, we include their entire datasets. We balance the general web data by sampling an equal number of tokens per language, upsampling Indonesion, Swahili and Welsh as needed to match their proportions in TransWebEdu. We then merge it with TransWebEdu at an nearly 1:11:1 ratio for continued pretraining. Building on TransWebLLM, we extend training for an additional 20,80020,800 steps, processing approximately 9090B tokens during this phase, with general web data accounting for only around 4545B tokens (less than 3% of the total). We refer to this continued pretraining model as TransWebLLM-web, as detailed in Table 4.

The 5-shot evaluation results of TransWebLLM-web on multilingual understanding and commonsense reasoning benchmarks (as detailed in Section 3.1) are in Table 3. TransWebLLM-web demonstrates a significant improvement over TransWebLLM, achieving higher average performance in general. Across all languages and non-English languages, TransWebLLM-web ranks as the top-performing model among all LLMs (45.9145.91 and 44.7744.77). For high-resource languages, TransWebLLM-web achieves performance comparable to EuroLLM, despite the latter being trained on 4T tokens. Notably, for Indonesian, TransWebLLM-web emerges as the best-performing model (49.7549.75), surpassing Southeast Asian-specific baselines such as Sailor and Sailor2. These results highlight the benefits of incorporating even a limited amount of general web data during continued pretraining for multilingual understanding and reasoning tasks.

Beyond understanding and reasoning tasks, we also evaluate the model’s linguistic proficiency, focusing on its ability to understand and generate coherent, grammatically accurate sentences. Faysse et al. (2024) introduced the fr-grammar and fr-vocabulary test sets in French to assess models’ grammar and vocabulary capabilities through structured language evaluations. We test both TransWebLLM and TransWebLLM-web on these benchmarks in a 5-shot setting to measure their proficiency in French linguistic competence. As shown in Table 5, TransWebLLM-web outperforms TransWebLLM by nearly 1010 accuracy points (74.7974.79 vs. 65.1365.13), demonstrating that even a small addition of general web data (4545B) in continued pretraining can significantly enhance linguistic proficiency.

Local culture reasoning provides a natural representation of causal reasoning within specific cultural contexts. COPAL-ID (Wibowo et al., 2023) is an Indonesian causal commonsense reasoning dataset, written by native speakers from scratch with standard Indonesian and Jakartan Indonesian, a widely spoken dialect in daily conversations. We evaluate both TransWebLLM and TransWebLLM-web on this benchmark in a 5-shot setting to assess their ability to reason within the Indonesian cultural sphere. As shown in Table 6, TransWebLLM-web improves Indonesian cultural reasoning by over an averaged 8 accuracy points (57.6157.61 vs. 48.8448.84) by incorporating a limited amount of general web data (4545B) in continued pretraining on TransWebLLM. It surpasses all LLM baselines except Sailor and Sailor2, which have been specifically trained for Indonesian.

Yang et al. (2023) shows that rephrasing MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021) samples enhances model reasoning performance across various domains. Motivated by these findings, we explore the impact of rephrased synthetic data on TransWebLLM. Instead of rephrasing MMLU test cases (Yang et al., 2023), we rephrase English web data into a multiple-choice (MC) style using an LLM, aligning with reasoning structure while maintaining its open-ended nature. We extract 1010BT English data from SlimPajama (Soboleva et al., 2023), generate 88BT MC synthetic data using Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1111111We use the prompt template as ”Write multiple-choice questions and answers based on the document: [doc]”., and upsample and integrate it into TransWebEdu with general web data, ensuring MC data constitutes 5% of the corpus. Given the improved performance of TransWebLLM-web, we continue pretraining for 9,000 steps, processing 4040B tokens, including 2B from MC data.

Prior works Faysse et al. (2024); Zhang et al. (2024); Martins et al. (2024) highlights the importance of a cooldown phase for enhancing model capabilities. While TransWebEdu emphasizes educational content, it lacks code and instruction data, such as question-answering (QA), compared to other LLMs. To address this, we introduce cooldown data during this phase: Python-Edu Ben Allal et al. (2024), an educational Python dataset from The Stack (4B tokens), and WebInstruct (Yue et al., 2024), a curated QA dataset (0.8B tokens) from the web. They are up-sampled and mixed with the previous-stage data (Table 4), forming 30% of the total. The model undergoes an additional 2020B-token training phase using a reduced learning rate121212We apply a constant learning rate schedule: 6×10−46\times 10^{-4} for earlier pretraining phases and 6×10−56\times 10^{-5} for cooldown.. Notably, cooldown data constitutes less than 66B tokens, accounting for only 0.3%0.3\% of total training tokens. We denote this final cooldown-trained model as TransWebLLM-cool.

We evaluate TransWebLLM-cool on all benchmarks in Sections 3.1 and 4.2.1, as well as Global-MMLU (Singh et al., 2024), covering nine languages (excluding Welsh), in a 55-shot setting. As shown in Table 7 for MMLU, TransWebLLM-cool, trained with additional rephrased synthetic and cooldown data, ranks among the top three models overall and achieves the highest performance in Swahili. Furthermore, Table 14 in Appendix H shows that TransWebLLM-cool surpasses TransWebLLM-web across all languages for understanding and reasoning tasks, ranking as the top LLM on average. Remarkably, it is the best-performing LLM for Arabic, Italian, Indonesian, Swahili, and Welsh. Additionally, Tables 15 and 16 in Appendix H show that TransWebLLM-cool, despite being trained with limited additional special data, improves both French linguistic proficiency and Indonesian cultural reasoning. These findings highlight the effectiveness of rephrased synthetic and cooldown data in enhancing multilingual pretraining based on NLLB-translated data.

We introduce TransWebEdu, a multilingual dataset generated through machine translation of a high-quality English source dataset. Our model, TransWebLLM, trained from scratch on this data, achieves competitive performance across nine non-English understanding and reasoning benchmarks, matching or surpassing state-of-the-art multilingual LLMs trained on closed data, such as Llama3.2, Qwen2.5, and Gemma on average. Furthermore, we demonstrate that incorporating less than 5% of TransWebEdu as domain-specific pretraining data establishes new state-of-the-art results in Arabic, Italian, Indonesian, Swahili, and Welsh for understanding and commonsense reasoning tasks. These findings highlight that constructing multilingual pretraining corpora using sentence-level translations can yield robust performance in multilingual LLMs. Our approach provides a scalable and efficient solution for creating multilingual pretraining data, particularly for medium- and low-resource languages, contributing to advancements in multilingual NLP research.
Our study yields promising results while also identifying areas for future exploration.

TransWebLLM, trained on TransWebEdu, achieves significant performance gains across 10 multilingual benchmarks. Further improvements are observed with the addition of general web data, rephrased synthetic data, and code and web-instruct data. However, due to computational constraints, we did not conduct ablation studies to determine the optimal data mixing ratios beyond pretraining with TransWebEdu. Future work will extend the “Beyond Pretraining with Translation Data” experiments in Section 4.2 to explore optimal data integration strategies from diverse sources for TransWebEdu.

In addition, our experiments focus on TransWebLLM, a 1.31.3B-parameter model that has shown promising results at this scale. However, it remains unclear whether the benefits of our translated pretraining data would persist or amplify in substantially larger models (e.g., 7070B+ parameters). Scaling up could provide deeper insights into multilingual learning dynamics and data efficiency. Future research will explore these aspects to validate and enhance the scalability of our multilingual pretraining approach.
This appendix provides additional technical details on our approach and supplementary evaluation results for the main paper.

[Table: Table 9: Model and pretraining hyperparameters.]

We employ Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1131313hf.co/mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 as our translation model. However, its efficacy when prompted for document-level translation, particularly with long-context English source documents, has not yet been verified. A recent related work by Maini et al. (2024) has empirically demonstrated that prompting an LLM to rephrase more than 300300 tokens could lead to information loss when rephrasing web data.

Following their setup, we first segment the English source documents from the sample-100100BT subset of FineWeb-Edu into shorter pieces, prompt Mistral to translate these segments sequentially, and subsequently reconstruct the whole translated document by concatenating the translated segments.The detailed translation pipeline is shown in Figure 2.

Adhering to the instruction format specified141414hf.co/mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 for Mistral-7B-Instruct, the chat template employed to prompt Mistral model for translation (using English-French as an example) is illustrated in Figure 3151515The highlighted portions in the template are adjusted according to the target language.. To maintain translation integrity, any sentence not fully translated to a terminal punctuation is omitted, based on the NLTK sentence tokenizer (Bird et al., 2009).

We translate English documents from FineWeb-Edu (Lozhkov et al., 2024) into three major European languages: French, German, and Spanish via prompting the Mistral-7B-Intruct model. To optimize memory efficiency and accelerate the inference process of Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1, we employ vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023), a library specifically designed for efficient large language model inference and serving. Using this setup, we translate approximately 5454 million English documents (a subset of sample-100100B of FineWeb-Edu) into the three target languages by prompting Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1. Table 12 presents the statistics of the original English data and the translated French, German, and Spanish. Leveraging vLLM’s efficiency, we estimate the total computational cost to be approximately 6.03×10226.03\times 10^{22} FLOPs.

Table 13 compares the performance of the model trained on Mistral-generated translation data (CuatroLLM) with the model trained on NLLB-generated data (TransWebLLM-4) across English, German, French, and Spanish benchmarks.
[Figure: Figure 2: Step-by-step illustration of the translation pipeline with the Mistral-7B-Instruct model.]
[Figure: Figure 3: Chat template used for prompting Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1 for English-French translation.]
[Table: Table 12: Statistics of Translation Data generated with the Mistral-7B-Model, measured in Llama2 tokenizer.]
[Table: Table 13: Performance comparison between CuatroLLM trained on LLM-translated data and TransWebLLM-4 across four selected languages. ]

We use the English, French, German, Italian, and Spanish subsets of the RedPajama-v2 (RPv2) Weber et al. (2024) as web data. Given that web data is inherently noisy, we make further use of the quality signals provided for RPv2 and filter each subset down to a smaller, high-quality subset. Specifically, we use the six most recent dumps from 2022 and 2023 and apply quality filtering using the Gopher rules Rae et al. (2021). Additionally, web data often contains near duplicates, stemming from boilerplate text, ads, and other computer-generated text that only differs by a few words, and removing these has been shown to positively affect training efficiency and reduce the amount of memorization Lee et al. (2021). We therefore adopt the MinHash algorithm with locality-sensitive hashing Broder (1997) to perform near-deduplication. We identify documents as near duplicates if their Jaccard similarity is greater than 0.80.8 and use 128128 hash functions.

The evaluation results of TransWebLLM-cool on understanding and reasoning, French linguistic proficiency, and reasoning for Indonesian local culture are presented in Table 14, 15, and 16, respectively.

[Table: Table 14: LLM performance across ten languages, categorized by resource availability and measured in accuracy. The last three columns report average results for all languages (All), non-English languages (Non-Eng), and high-resource languages (High). The top three models are underlined and the best score for each language is highlighted.]

[Table: Table 15: French grammar and vocabulary proficiency evaluation for TransWebLLM-cool, measured in accuracy. The last column reports the task average score. Models with ∗ denote regional models trained with support for French.]

[Table: Table 16: COPAL-ID evaluation for TransWebLLM-cool, measured in accuracy. The last column reports task average score. Models with ∗ denote regional models trained with support for Indonesian.]
